intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a friendly, conversational tone over clinical health language
facade.axisTargeted: voice and personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Casual check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard copy should use warmth, humor, and everyday language (e.g., 'How's your sleep game?' instead of 'Sleep duration logged')
facade.rejectImplies: Users expect clinical precision and formal tone; friendly language may feel dismissive of serious health tracking

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer a friendly, conversational tone over clinical health language
evidence.implication: Dashboard copy should use warmth, humor, and everyday language (e.g., 'How's your sleep game?' instead of 'Sleep duration logged')

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer high contrast and clarity for health data readability, with dark surfaces reducing eye strain during frequent check-ins
facade.label: Dark cards, light text
facade.acceptImplies: Explore dark-mode-first design with crisp white typography; test if dark backgrounds make metrics feel more legible and authoritative
facade.rejectImplies: Lean toward light or mixed backgrounds; dark may feel heavy or clinical for a personal wellness tool; test warmer, softer contrast instead

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer immediate numeric feedback (counts, badges, changes) that appear without transition, creating snappy confirmation of action.
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — instant feedback vs. animated transitions
facade.label: Instant number pop-ups
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should use instant appearance of metric updates, badge notifications, and numeric changes with no fade-in or slide animation.
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should favor smooth animated transitions when metrics update, numbers slide in, or badges fade-in gradually rather than popping instantly.

facade.hypothesis: Users want to keep experiment categories permanently visible and accessible, reducing navigation clicks and supporting quick context switching between different health tracking areas
facade.axisTargeted: Sidebar vs tabs for primary navigation structure
facade.label: Sidebar navigation
facade.acceptImplies: Explore persistent left-panel layouts with collapsible sections for experiment types, metrics hierarchies, and filtering—treat sidebar as the spine of the dashboard
facade.rejectImplies: Explore top-level tabs or hamburger menus instead; test whether horizontal navigation or full-screen focus areas better suit the workflow of long-term experiment tracking

facade.hypothesis: The user prefers a calming, gentle visual palette that reduces cognitive load and feels meditative rather than stimulating
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool + calm vs energetic
facade.label: Soft, muted colors
facade.acceptImplies: Design should use desaturated, earthy tones; soft blues, grays, warm beiges. Avoid bright primaries. Supports intimate, introspective mood.
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer higher contrast, bolder accents, or more energetic visual intensity. Leans toward cooler, crisper, or more vivid color language.

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards often want prominent metric displays (weight, blood pressure, step count) but struggle to know whether to show raw values alone or pair them with trend indicators, targets, or historical context
facade.axisTargeted: Data density vs. clarity — whether to lead with isolated key metrics or embed them in comparative/contextual information
facade.label: Big numbers, tiny context
facade.acceptImplies: The user gravitates toward bold, prominent single metrics with minimal surrounding information; their dashboard prioritizes at-a-glance scanning over narrative interpretation
facade.rejectImplies: The user wants metrics embedded in richer context (trends, goals, ranges); they value story-telling through data relationships over isolated number prominence

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users want quantified feedback and visual data representation rather than narrative-only updates
facade.axisTargeted: Data visualization specificity — numeric/graphical vs descriptive/narrative
facade.label: Progress bars and numbers
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should emphasize charts, metrics, and progress visualization; users trust numbers over stories
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should use qualitative language and story-based reflections; metrics alone feel cold or anxiety-inducing

draft.title: Experiment Tracker Dashboard
draft.summary: A personal health dashboard for monitoring long-term experiments with visual progress tracking, experiment cards, and key metrics at a glance.
